summary: EAGL/OpenGL ES rendering fails on iOS with .NET 10 MAUI — SKGLView produces white canvas with 'Failed to bind EAGLDrawable' errors because the iOS handler still uses deprecated EAGLContext instead of Metal.

stepsToReproduce: Create a .NET 10 MAUI iOS app that uses SKGLView (e.g., Mapsui MapControl)
Run on an iOS device or simulator
Observe white canvas and EAGL error messages in console

repoLinks.description: Mapsui MapControl using SKGLView for MAUI rendering

bugSignals.errorMessage: Failed to bind EAGLDrawable: <CAEAGLLayer> to GL_RENDERBUFFER 1; Failed to make complete multisample framebuffer object 8cd6

versionAnalysis.relevanceReason: The iOS SKGLViewHandler still uses EAGL-based SKGLView. The underlying EAGL/GLKit framework was deprecated by Apple in iOS 12 and .NET 10 may have tightened support.

regression.reason: Reporter states this occurs only on .NET 10, not .NET 9. This suggests the .NET 10 iOS SDK or runtime changes affected EAGL/GLKit behavior rather than a SkiaSharp code change.

analysis.summary: The iOS MAUI SKGLViewHandler uses EAGLContext (OpenGL ES 2) via GLKView, which Apple deprecated in iOS 12.0. The MacCatalyst handler already uses Metal via SKMetalView. On .NET 10, EAGL rendering appears broken — the CAEAGLLayer fails to bind to the GL renderbuffer, producing a white canvas. The fix is to migrate the iOS handler to use SKMetalView (which already exists and works on iOS) like the MacCatalyst handler does.
analysis.rationale: Classified as type/bug because rendering produces a blank white canvas with console errors — this is clearly broken behavior, not a usage question. The area is SkiaSharp.Views.Maui because the issue is in the platform handler layer. The root cause is using a deprecated Apple API (EAGL/GLKit) that .NET 10 may no longer fully support. SKMetalView already exists for iOS but the MAUI handler doesn't use it.

keySignals.text: Failed to bind EAGLDrawable: <CAEAGLLayer: 0x600001767a40> to GL_RENDERBUFFER 1
keySignals.interpretation: EAGL framebuffer setup fails — the CAEAGLLayer cannot create a renderbuffer. This is a low-level OpenGL ES/iOS framework failure.
Regression specific to .NET 10, not a SkiaSharp version change. .NET 10 targets newer Xcode/iOS SDK which may have tightened EAGL deprecation.
SkiaSharp already marks this as deprecated and recommends Metal. The iOS handler needs to be migrated.

codeInvestigation.finding: iOS MAUI handler uses SKGLView (EAGL/OpenGL ES based GLKView). Creates MauiSKGLView which inherits from the EAGL-based SKGLView.
SKGLView is marked ObsoletedOSPlatform for ios12.0. Uses EAGLContext with OpenGLES2, GLKViewDrawableColorFormat.RGBA8888, and 4x multisample — all deprecated APIs.
MacCatalyst handler already uses SKMetalView instead of SKGLView. This is the pattern the iOS handler should follow.
SKMetalView exists for iOS (__IOS__ conditional). Uses MTKView with Metal backend. Already compiles and works on iOS — just not wired up in the MAUI handler.

nextQuestions: Does .NET 10 explicitly remove or break GLKit/EAGL bindings, or is this a side effect of targeting a newer iOS SDK?
Can the iOS handler be switched to Metal without breaking existing apps that explicitly depend on OpenGL ES behavior?
Is this reproducible on all iOS devices/simulators with .NET 10, or only specific hardware?

resolution.hypothesis: The .NET 10 iOS SDK or runtime no longer properly supports EAGLContext/GLKit, causing framebuffer binding failures. The SKMetalView already exists and works on iOS but the MAUI iOS handler hasn't been migrated to use it (unlike MacCatalyst which already uses Metal).

proposals.title: Use SKCanvasView instead of SKGLView
proposals.description: Switch from SKGLView (GPU-accelerated via EAGL) to SKCanvasView (CPU rendering). This avoids the EAGL path entirely. The tradeoff is lower performance for GPU-intensive rendering, but it works reliably on all iOS versions.

proposals.title: Migrate iOS SKGLViewHandler to use SKMetalView
proposals.description: Change SKGLViewHandler.iOS.cs to use SKMetalView (Metal backend) instead of SKGLView (EAGL backend), following the same pattern already used in SKGLViewHandler.MacCatalyst.cs. SKMetalView already compiles for iOS and supports the same rendering pipeline.

proposals.title: Custom MAUI handler mapping to SKMetalView
proposals.description: As a user-side workaround, create a custom SKGLViewHandler that maps to SKMetalView on iOS, using the MacCatalyst handler as a template. Register via ConfigureMauiHandlers in MauiProgram.cs.

resolution.recommendedReason: Simplest immediate workaround that avoids the deprecated EAGL path entirely. For long-term, the iOS handler should be migrated to Metal.

actionability.reason: The root cause (EAGL deprecation + .NET 10 change) is well-understood but the proper fix (migrating iOS handler to Metal) needs implementation and testing. The existing labels are correct.

actions.description: Add OpenGL backend label to existing labels
Post analysis with workaround (use SKCanvasView) and context about EAGL deprecation
actions.comment:
Thanks for the report and the error logs.

The root cause is that the iOS `SKGLView` uses Apple's deprecated `EAGLContext` (OpenGL ES), which appears to be broken under .NET 10's iOS SDK. The error "Failed to bind EAGLDrawable" confirms the OpenGL ES framebuffer can't be created.

Here's a workaround you can use while we investigate: switch from `SKGLView` to `SKCanvasView` in your Mapsui MapControl. `SKCanvasView` uses CPU rendering and doesn't depend on EAGL/OpenGL ES at all. The tradeoff is lower GPU-accelerated performance, but it avoids the broken EAGL path entirely.

For context: the MacCatalyst handler already uses Metal (`SKMetalView`) instead of EAGL. The iOS handler needs the same migration — `SKMetalView` already compiles and works on iOS, it just hasn't been wired up in the MAUI handler yet.

actions.description: Cross-reference old EAGL issue #646 (same error pattern)
